1→package cmd
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"github.com/spf13/cobra"
     5→)
     6→
     7→var initCmd = &cobra.Command{
     8→	Use:         "init [directory]",
     9→	Short:       "Initialize a nurge project",
    10→	Hidden:      true,
    11→	Args:        cobra.MaximumNArgs(1),
    12→	Annotations: map[string]string{"skipAuth": "true"},
    13→	RunE:        runInit,
    14→}
    15→
    16→func init() {
    17→	initCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&initForce, "force", false, "Re-run all steps even if already completed")
    18→	initCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&initSkipHooks, "skip-hooks", false, "Skip Claude Code hook installation")
    19→	initCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&initVerbose, "verbose", false, "Show detailed output")
    20→
    21→	rootCmd.AddCommand(initCmd)
    22→}
    23→

1→# Nurge Skills System
     2→
     3→This folder documents Nurge's skill system — the 17 instruction files that teach Claude how to handle specific outbound sales tasks, personalized to each user.
     4→
     5→## What Are Skills?
     6→
     7→Skills are plain-text instruction files that Claude reads at the start of relevant conversations. Each skill teaches Claude how to do one specific job — write a cold email, research a prospect, review weekly performance — using the user's actual company data, ideal customer profile, personas, and email patterns.
     8→
     9→Every Nurge user gets a unique set of skills generated from their own data. Two users selling different products to different audiences will have completely different skill content, even though the skill structure is the same.
    10→
    11→## How Many Skills?
    12→
    13→17 total:
    14→- 16 project-local skills stored in `.claude/skills/nurge-*/SKILL.md` within the user's project
    15→- 1 global setup skill stored in `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md` on the user's machine
    16→
    17→## Documents in This Folder
    18→
    19→| Document | What It Covers |
    20→|----------|---------------|
    21→| [how-skills-are-created.md](how-skills-are-created.md) | The end-to-end creation process: from setup conversation to generated skill files |
    22→| [skill-catalog.md](skill-catalog.md) | All 17 skills with purpose, behavior, config dependencies, and CLI usage |
    23→| [maturity-levels.md](maturity-levels.md) | How skills adapt based on the user's outbound maturity (zero, experimenting, operating, scaling) |
    24→| [versioning-and-learning.md](versioning-and-learning.md) | How skills improve over time, version tracking, rollback, and the learning system |
    25→| [config-reference.md](config-reference.md) | Every config key that skills read, what it stores, and which skills use it |
    26→

1→# Skill Catalog
     2→
     3→All 17 Nurge skills organized by category. Each entry covers what the skill does, what data it reads, what commands it runs, when it's used, and how it behaves at different maturity levels.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Setup Skill (Global)
     8→
     9→### nurge-setup
    10→
    11→**Location:** `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md` (global, installed to user's home directory)
    12→
    13→**Purpose:** The onboarding orchestrator. Runs the full two-phase setup process that connects services, gathers user data, and generates all other skills.
    14→
    15→**What it does:**
    16→- Checks prerequisites (project initialized, services connected)
    17→- Handles resume logic (can pick up where it left off if interrupted)
    18→- Runs Phase 1: Foundation (7 steps collecting name, company, role, tools, email connection, sync, maturity detection)
    19→- Detects maturity level and delegates to the appropriate Phase 2 flow
    20→- Phase 2 collects ICP, personas, positioning, KPIs, and generates all 16 project skills
    21→- Ends with a live demo showing the product in action
    22→
    23→**When it's used:** Once, during initial setup. Invoked by the user running `/nurge:setup` in Claude Code.
    24→
    25→**Not regenerated:** This is the only skill that stays fixed. It's installed by the Go binary during `nurge init` and updated when Nurge is updated.
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## Context Skills (3)
    30→
    31→These are the knowledge base. They hold static information about the user's company, target market, and buyer personas that all other skills reference.
    32→
    33→### nurge-product
    34→
    35→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-product/SKILL.md`
    36→
    37→**Purpose:** Everything Claude needs to know about the user's product to write accurate outbound emails.
    38→
    39→**What it contains:**
    40→- Company name, website, and industry
    41→- One-line product description for cold outreach
    42→- Value propositions (what problems it solves)
    43→- Key features and differentiators
    44→- Competitive positioning
    45→- Pricing model (if known)
    46→
    47→**Config it reads:** `company.website`, `company.description`, `company.industry`, `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`
    48→
    49→**How it varies by maturity:**
    50→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from website scrape and setup conversation. May be thin initially.
    51→- Operating/Scaling: Built from website scrape plus how the user actually describes the product in their sent emails. Often more grounded than the website copy — reveals what resonates with prospects.
    52→
    53→**When it's used:** Every time Claude drafts any outbound communication. It's the foundational product knowledge that all action skills reference.
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→### nurge-icp
    58→
    59→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-icp/SKILL.md`
    60→
    61→**Purpose:** Defines the ideal customer profile — which companies to target.
    62→
    63→**What it contains:**
    64→- Company size ranges (employees, revenue)
    65→- Industries and verticals
    66→- Company stage (startup, growth, enterprise)
    67→- Geography
    68→- Technographics (tech stack signals, if known)
    69→- Negative filters (who NOT to target)
    70→
    71→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `prospects.geography`
    72→
    73→**How it varies by maturity:**
    74→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from user assumptions and AI suggestions. Starts narrow (one segment) and expands as data comes in. Acknowledges uncertainty.
    75→- Operating/Scaling: Inferred from real email patterns — who they actually email vs. who responds vs. who converts. Often reveals misalignment between the user's stated targets and their actual best-performing segments.
    76→
    77→**When it's used:** By nurge-research for targeting, nurge-prospects for list building, nurge-qualify for fit scoring, and nurge-analytics for segment analysis.
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→### nurge-personas
    82→
    83→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-personas/SKILL.md`
    84→
    85→**Purpose:** Defines buyer personas — which people to message within target companies, and how to message each one differently.
    86→
    87→**What it contains (for each of 2-3 personas):**
    88→- Title and role patterns
    89→- What they care about (pain points, priorities)
    90→- Messaging angle (what resonates with this persona)
    91→- Tone preferences (technical vs. business, formal vs. casual)
    92→- Common objections
    93→- Whether they're a champion, decision-maker, or blocker
    94→
    95→**Config it reads:** `personas` (JSON array)
    96→
    97→**How it varies by maturity:**
    98→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from ICP and product type with AI suggestions. User confirms or edits.
    99→- Operating/Scaling: Extracted from email patterns — which titles they email most, which respond best, what messaging they use per role. Includes real reply rate data per persona.
   100→
   101→**When it's used:** By nurge-outbound to tailor email voice, nurge-sequence to vary cadence messaging, nurge-research to know who to look for at target companies, and nurge-analytics for per-persona performance breakdowns.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## Research Skills (2)
   106→
   107→These handle the intelligence-gathering phase before any outreach happens.
   108→
   109→### nurge-research
   110→
   111→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-research/SKILL.md`
   112→
   113→**Purpose:** Deep-dive research on a target company or prospect before writing any outreach.
   114→
   115→**What it does:**
   116→- Builds a complete prospect dossier using a multi-layered lookup strategy
   117→- Finds the right person at the target company (matching buyer personas)
   118→- Gets their contact info (email, phone)
   119→- Surfaces what they care about, recent activity, and trigger events
   120→- Detects competitive intel (are they using a competitor?)
   121→- Presents the dossier for user review before handing off to drafting
   122→
   123→**Research Strategy (5 parallel layers):**
   124→
   125→1. **Web Search (context layer)** — Person name + company for role and background. Company name + news for funding, hiring, product launches, trigger events. Cross-references results to avoid wrong-person errors.
   126→
   127→2. **Company Website (contact layer)** — Fetches the company site for positioning and team info. Geography-aware legal pages are goldmines for contact info:
   128→   - France: `/mentionslegales` or `/mentions-legales` (legally required — founder email, phone, registration)
   129→   - Germany/Austria: `/impressum` (legally required — managing director, email, phone)
   130→   - US/UK: `/about`, `/contact`, `/team` pages
   131→
   132→3. **Enrichment APIs (structured data layer)** — Optional integrations with ContactOut, Apollo, Clay for verified email, phone, and work history. The skill works without them but they add verified data.
   133→
   134→4. **Public Profiles (personality layer)** — LinkedIn posts (via web search), Twitter/X, GitHub, personal blogs, podcast appearances, conference talks. Reveals how the person thinks and communicates.
   135→
   136→5. **Prior Interactions (history layer)** — Checks whether the user has emailed this person or company before, and what happened.
   137→
   138→All 5 layers run as parallel agents simultaneously, then combine into a structured dossier.
   139→
   140→**The dossier includes:**
   141→- Person: name, role, background, what they care about
   142→- Contact: email (verified or best guess), phone, LinkedIn URL
   143→- Company: what they do, size, stage, recent news, trigger events
   144→- Messaging angle: based on persona match and what this specific person cares about
   145→- Confidence level: flags low-confidence findings
   146→
   147→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `personas`, `product.positioning`, `prospects.geography`
   148→
   149→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (check prior interactions)
   150→
   151→**Critical rule:** Always present research to the user for review before drafting. Wrong info in a personalized email is worse than a generic email.
   152→
   153→---
   154→
   155→### nurge-qualify
   156→
   157→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-qualify/SKILL.md`
   158→
   159→**Purpose:** Evaluate whether a lead is worth pursuing and classify their relationship type.
   160→
   161→**What it does (two-step process):**
   162→
   163→**Step 1 — Classify the lead:**
   164→
   165→| Classification | Meaning | Next Action |
   166→|---|---|---|
   167→| Prospect | Fits ICP, could be a customer | Continue to qualification scoring |
   168→| Competitor | Sells the same or similar thing | Flag and disqualify. Never email. Log for competitive intel. |
   169→| Partnership | Complementary product or service | Flag as potential partner. Route to partnership messaging, not sales. |
   170→
   171→Classification compares what the prospect's company does vs. what the user's company does: same space + same buyer = competitor; same space + different buyer = partnership; different space + matches ICP = prospect.
   172→
   173→**Step 2 — For prospects, run the qualification framework:**
   174→- Apply BANT (or custom framework) from config
   175→- Score fit against ICP criteria
   176→- Check persona match
   177→- Assess buying and timing signals
   178→- Check for existing relationship (prior emails)
   179→- Recommend: pursue (strong fit + timing), nurture (good fit, bad timing), or pass (poor fit)
   180→
   181→**Config it reads:** `qualification.framework`, `icp.*`, `personas`, `company.description`, `company.industry`
   182→
   183→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (prior interaction history)
   184→
   185→**How it varies by maturity:**
   186→- Zero/Experimenting: Uses BANT or the framework set during setup. Conservative — defaults to "nurture" when unsure.
   187→- Operating/Scaling: Framework informed by pipeline data (deal velocity, win rates) if CRM is connected.
   188→
   189→**When it's used:** After research, before committing to a sequence. Prevents wasting sends on bad-fit prospects, competitors, and misrouted partnerships.
   190→
   191→---
   192→
   193→## Workflow Skill (1)
   194→
   195→### nurge-pipeline
   196→
   197→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-pipeline/SKILL.md`
   198→
   199→**Purpose:** The orchestrator. Manages the full outbound workflow and coordinates all other skills.
   200→
   201→**What it does:**
   202→- Manages prospect pipeline stages: new, researched, qualified, sequencing, replied, meeting, deal
   203→- Decides which skill to invoke at each stage
   204→- Tracks where every prospect is in the funnel
   205→- Suggests next actions ("You have 5 prospects waiting for follow-up, 3 new companies to research")
   206→- Enforces daily sending limits from KPI targets
   207→
   208→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`, `sending.*`
   209→
   210→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list`, `nurge emails stats`, `nurge config show`
   211→
   212→**When it's used:** When the user asks "what should I do today?" or "show me my pipeline." The starting point for a typical outbound session.
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## Action Skills (5)
   217→
   218→These do the actual work — writing emails, managing sequences, handling replies, and booking meetings.
   219→
   220→### nurge-outbound
   221→
   222→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-outbound/SKILL.md`
   223→
   224→**Purpose:** Write cold outbound emails. This is the highest-priority action skill.
   225→
   226→**What it does:**
   227→- Drafts personalized cold emails using product knowledge, ICP, persona-specific messaging, and research findings
   228→- Enforces a strict 50-90 word limit. Words are counted before presenting. If over 90, cut ruthlessly. No exceptions.
   229→- Follows a rigid structure: 1 personalized hook (1 sentence) then 1 value prop (1-2 sentences) then 1 CTA (1 sentence). Nothing else.
   230→- Eliminates all filler: no "I hope this finds you well", no company backstory, no feature lists, no "I'd love to", no "I was wondering if"
   231→- Matches tone to persona (technical for CTOs, business for VPs)
   232→- If social proof exists in config: weaves ONE relevant proof point naturally. Never fabricates proof.
   233→- If no social proof exists: skips entirely — never invents it.
   234→- Includes compliance requirements based on prospect geography (unsubscribe for CAN-SPAM, physical address if required)
   235→- Presents draft with word count for user review before sending
   236→
   237→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `personas`, `prospects.geography`, `sending.*`
   238→
   239→**CLI commands:** `nurge send --to <email> --subject <s> --body <b>`
   240→
   241→**Key principle:** Never sends without user approval. Always presents the draft first.
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→### nurge-sequence
   246→
   247→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-sequence/SKILL.md`
   248→
   249→**Purpose:** Design and manage multi-step outreach cadences.
   250→
   251→**What it does:**
   252→- Creates sequences: first touch (Day 1), follow-up (Day 3), final nudge (Day 7)
   253→- Varies messaging per step — each touch uses a new angle, not "just following up"
   254→- Adapts sequence length to persona and deal complexity
   255→- Tracks sequence progress per prospect
   256→- Suggests templates based on performance data
   257→
   258→**Config it reads:** `personas`, `kpi.*`, `sending.*`
   259→
   260→**How it varies by maturity:**
   261→- Zero/Experimenting: Generates simple 3-touch sequences based on best-practice frameworks.
   262→- Operating/Scaling: Sequences informed by real performance data — what cadence patterns actually get replies.
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→### nurge-followup
   267→
   268→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-followup/SKILL.md`
   269→
   270→**Purpose:** Write follow-up emails for prospects who haven't replied.
   271→
   272→**What it does:**
   273→- Drafts follow-ups that add new value (never "just checking in")
   274→- References the original email naturally
   275→- Escalates the angle: different pain point, new social proof, or a more direct ask
   276→- Respects sequence timing
   277→- Knows when to stop (after final touch, marks as "no response")
   278→
   279→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `personas`
   280→
   281→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (pull original thread), `nurge send`
   282→
   283→---
   284→
   285→### nurge-replies
   286→
   287→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-replies/SKILL.md`
   288→
   289→**Purpose:** Handle incoming replies — positive, negative, objections, or referrals.
   290→
   291→**What it does:**
   292→- Classifies reply intent: interested, objection, not now, not interested, referral, out-of-office
   293→- Drafts the appropriate response for each type:
   294→  - Interested: move to booking
   295→  - Objection: address with relevant value prop
   296→  - Not now: schedule nurture follow-up
   297→  - Not interested: graceful exit, respect the no
   298→  - Referral: thank and follow up with the referral
   299→- Flags tone issues (if reply is angry, approaches carefully)
   300→
   301→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `personas`, `qualification.framework`
   302→
   303→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge send`
   304→
   305→---
   306→
   307→### nurge-book
   308→
   309→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-book/SKILL.md`
   310→
   311→**Purpose:** Convert positive replies into booked meetings.
   312→
   313→**What it does:**
   314→- Proposes specific times (not "when works for you?")
   315→- Handles timezone differences
   316→- Drafts a booking confirmation email
   317→- Prepares a meeting brief: company research, persona context, talking points, potential objections
   318→- Sends reminders before the meeting
   319→- Handles reschedules and no-shows gracefully
   320→
   321→**Config it reads:** `personas`, `product.positioning`
   322→
   323→**CLI commands:** `nurge send`
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## Prospect Management Skill (1)
   328→
   329→### nurge-prospects
   330→
   331→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-prospects/SKILL.md`
   332→
   333→**Purpose:** Build and manage prospect lists. For users without a CRM, this IS their CRM.
   334→
   335→**What it does:**
   336→- Builds targeted prospect lists from ICP and persona criteria
   337→- Segments lists by persona, industry, company size, geography
   338→- Tracks prospect status: new, researched, qualified, contacted, replied, meeting, deal, passed
   339→- Manages suppression: who to stop emailing (opted out, bounced, bad fit, already a customer)
   340→- Deduplicates contacts
   341→- Imports from CSV if user has existing lists
   342→- Surfaces prospects that need attention
   343→
   344→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `personas`, `prospects.geography`
   345→
   346→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge emails stats`
   347→
   348→**How it varies by maturity:**
   349→- Zero/Experimenting: This is critical — Nurge is their only system for managing prospects. Must handle list building, segmentation, and status tracking end to end.
   350→- Operating/Scaling: Less critical if they have a CRM, but still useful for Nurge-specific tracking and for users whose CRM isn't yet supported.
   351→
   352→**When it's used:** "Build me a list of 50 CTOs at Series B SaaS companies" or "who haven't I followed up with?" or "show me my prospect pipeline."
   353→
   354→---
   355→
   356→## Health Skills (2)
   357→
   358→These protect the user's sending reputation and data quality.
   359→
   360→### nurge-hygiene
   361→
   362→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-hygiene/SKILL.md`
   363→
   364→**Purpose:** Keep data clean and outbound compliant.
   365→
   366→**What it does:**
   367→- Flags stale prospects (no activity in X weeks)
   368→- Identifies bounced and invalid emails
   369→- Checks for duplicate contacts
   370→- Enforces suppression lists (unsubscribes, bounces)
   371→- Verifies email formats before sending
   372→- Flags compliance issues based on prospect geography
   373→- Suggests list cleanup actions
   374→
   375→**Config it reads:** `prospects.geography`
   376→
   377→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list`, `nurge emails stats`
   378→
   379→**When it's used:** Periodically — triggered by nurge-pipeline or nurge-review. Also runs before any bulk send.
   380→
   381→---
   382→
   383→### nurge-sending-health
   384→
   385→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-sending-health/SKILL.md`
   386→
   387→**Purpose:** Monitor and manage email sending infrastructure, including warmup.
   388→
   389→**What it does:**
   390→- Checks DNS records (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) and flags issues with fix instructions
   391→- Monitors sending volume against daily limits
   392→- Tracks bounce rates and spam complaint signals
   393→- Manages warmup schedule for fresh domains:
   394→  - Week 1-2: 5 emails/day
   395→  - Week 3-4: 10-15 emails/day
   396→  - Week 5+: 20-30 emails/day (if health stats are clean)
   397→  - Auto-reduces if bounce rate spikes
   398→- Recommends dedicated outbound domain if user sends from their primary domain
   399→- Alerts when something looks wrong
   400→
   401→**Config it reads:** `sending.domain_type`, `sending.mailbox_count`, `sending.warmup_status`, `kpi.daily_emails`
   402→
   403→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `dig TXT` (DNS checks)
   404→
   405→**Future enhancement:** When warmup service connectors are added (Instantly, MailReach), this skill will pull real reputation data and auto-adjust limits based on actual reputation scores instead of time-based schedules.
   406→
   407→---
   408→
   409→## Measure Skills (2)
   410→
   411→These track performance and help the user improve over time.
   412→
   413→### nurge-analytics
   414→
   415→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-analytics/SKILL.md`
   416→
   417→**Purpose:** Track outbound performance against KPI targets.
   418→
   419→**What it does:**
   420→- Dashboard: emails sent, reply rate, positive replies, meetings booked vs. KPI targets
   421→- Breakdown by persona, segment, and sequence
   422→- Trend analysis: is performance improving or declining?
   423→- Surfaces best and worst performing: subject lines, personas, send times, sequences
   424→- Compares against benchmarks from the setup KPI conversation
   425→- Funnel analysis: sent to replied to meeting to deal conversion rates
   426→
   427→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`
   428→
   429→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge context generate --format json`
   430→
   431→**How it varies by maturity:**
   432→- Zero/Experimenting: Limited to basic volume metrics and reply tracking.
   433→- Operating/Scaling: Full breakdown by persona, segment, sequence, send time, and subject line patterns.
   434→
   435→**When it's used:** "How am I doing?" or "show me my analytics" or triggered by nurge-review during periodic check-ins.
   436→
   437→---
   438→
   439→### nurge-review
   440→
   441→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-review/SKILL.md`
   442→
   443→**Purpose:** Periodic coaching — weekly or monthly review of outbound performance with actionable advice.
   444→
   445→**What it does:**
   446→- Pulls KPI progress: "This week you sent X emails, got Y replies, booked Z meetings (target was W)"
   447→- Highlights wins: "Your CTO emails are getting 18% reply rate — above target"
   448→- Flags problems: "VP Eng reply rate dropped to 3% — let's adjust the messaging"
   449→- Compares current period to previous period
   450→- Suggests specific actions: increase CTO volume, rewrite VP Eng sequences, try a new subject line angle
   451→- Updates KPIs if current targets are too easy or too hard
   452→- Feeds insights back into other skills (adjust persona messaging, ICP focus)
   453→
   454→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`, `user.name`, `personas`
   455→
   456→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `nurge context generate --format json`, `nurge learn list --format json`
   457→
   458→**How it varies by maturity:**
   459→- Zero/Experimenting: Lighter reviews focused on getting started and building habits.
   460→- Operating: Full KPI review with data-driven recommendations.
   461→- Scaling: Includes per-rep breakdowns showing who's hitting targets and who needs help, with best-practice sharing from top performers.
   462→
   463→**When it's used:** User asks "give me a weekly review" or nurge-pipeline suggests it's time for a check-in.
   464→
   465→---

   482→
   483→## Skill Interaction Map
   484→
   485→Skills don't operate in isolation. Here's how they reference each other:
   486→
   487→- **nurge-pipeline** orchestrates all other skills and decides which to invoke
   488→- **nurge-research** feeds into **nurge-qualify** which feeds into **nurge-outbound**
   489→- **nurge-outbound** produces emails that **nurge-sequence** manages as multi-step cadences
   490→- **nurge-followup** handles non-responses within sequences
   491→- **nurge-replies** classifies responses and routes interested replies to **nurge-book**
   492→- **nurge-prospects** provides the lead lists that **nurge-research** investigates
   493→- **nurge-hygiene** and **nurge-sending-health** protect deliverability for all sending skills
   494→- **nurge-analytics** and **nurge-review** measure results and suggest improvements to all other skills
   495→- **nurge-product**, **nurge-icp**, and **nurge-personas** are referenced by nearly every other skill as the knowledge foundation
   496→

1→# How Skills Are Created
     2→
     3→Skills are generated during the Nurge setup process — a two-phase onboarding that first gathers data about the user, then generates personalized instruction files from that data.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## The Two-Phase Setup
     8→
     9→### Phase 1: Foundation (All Users)
    10→
    11→Phase 1 is the same regardless of user maturity. It collects the signals needed to understand who the user is and what they have to work with. Seven steps, asked one at a time as a conversation:
    12→
    13→**F1: Name** — "What should I call you?" The user's name is used throughout all skills for a personal touch.
    14→
    15→**F2: Company Discovery** — "What's your website?" Claude fetches the website and extracts: what they sell, their market, positioning, and social proof (customer logos, testimonials, metrics, case studies). The user confirms or corrects. If the website is unreachable, the user describes their company in a few sentences instead.
    16→
    17→**F3: Role and Team** — "What's your role?" and "Are you solo or do you have a team?" This determines whether they're a founder, head of sales, SDR, or AE — and whether they're working alone or managing a team. Team size is a key signal for maturity detection.
    18→
    19→**F4: Tool Inventory** — Three specific questions about their current stack:
    20→1. Do you use a CRM? (HubSpot, Salesforce, Pipedrive, other, none)
    21→2. Do you use a sequence tool? (Instantly, Lemlist, Smartlead, Apollo, other, none)
    22→3. Do you use enrichment tools? (Apollo, Clay, ContactOut, other, none)
    23→
    24→**F5: Connect Email** — Connects Gmail via OAuth. This is always done regardless of other factors — the email connection is how Nurge learns. If connection fails, setup continues with conversation-only data.
    25→
    26→**F6: Sync and Classify** — After getting privacy consent ("Nurge will send email subjects and snippets to Claude for classification. No full bodies. Continue?"), syncs the last 90 days of sent emails and classifies every one into categories: outbound, internal, personal, transactional, newsletter. For uncertain emails (~5%), the user is asked. The outbound count becomes a key detection signal.
    27→
    28→**F7: Maturity Detection** — An algorithm determines the user's maturity level based on three signals:
    29→
    30→| Signal | How It's Used |
    31→|--------|--------------|
    32→| Outbound email count | Under 200 = limited data. 200-499 = growing. 500+ = rich data. |
    33→| Has a CRM | Indicates some process maturity |
    34→| Team size | Solo vs. team determines Operating vs. Scaling |
    35→
    36→The detection result is presented to the user in plain language for confirmation, and they can override it.
    37→
    38→### Phase 2: Maturity-Specific Setup
    39→
    40→Based on the detected maturity level, the user enters one of four Phase 2 flows. Each flow collects the same categories of information (ICP, personas, positioning, KPIs) but does so differently depending on how much data exists.
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## The Four Phase 2 Flows
    45→
    46→### Zero (No outbound history, no tools)
    47→
    48→**Who gets here:** Users with fewer than 200 outbound emails and no CRM. Starting from scratch.
    49→
    50→**Philosophy:** Infrastructure before messaging. Validate deliverability before sending anything.
    51→
    52→**12 steps (Z1-Z12):**
    53→
    54→1. **Sending Infrastructure** — Asks about domain type (dedicated vs. primary), mailbox count, and warmup status. Sets conservative daily limits (5/day for fresh domains). Warns about risks of sending from a primary business domain.
    55→
    56→2. **Deliverability Check** — Runs DNS checks for SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records. This gates everything — no point writing great emails if they land in spam. Provides fix instructions for any issues found.
    57→
    58→3. **Channel Reality** — Honest disclosure: Nurge handles email only. LinkedIn and phone are on the roadmap.
    59→
    60→4. **Prospect Geography** — Where prospects are located. Sets compliance context silently (GDPR, CAN-SPAM) without lecturing.
    61→
    62→5. **ICP from Assumptions** — Since there's no email data, the ICP is built from the user's description of their ideal customers combined with what was learned from their website. Starts narrow (one segment) with the expectation it will expand as data comes in.
    63→
    64→6. **Buyer Personas** — 2-3 personas suggested by Claude based on ICP and product type. For each: title, pain points, messaging angle. The user confirms or edits.
    65→
    66→7. **Product Positioning** — A punchy, under-15-word one-liner for cold outreach, derived from the website scrape. Iterated with the user until it clicks.
    67→
    68→8. **Social Proof** — Checks website data for proof points (logos, testimonials, metrics). If found, the user curates which to use. If none exist, saved as "none" — skills never fabricate proof.
    69→
    70→9. **Qualification Framework** — A lightweight BANT framework (Budget, Authority, Need, Timing) suggested as a default. The user can edit.
    71→
    72→10. **Outbound Goals and KPIs** — Conservative targets based on infrastructure constraints. A fresh domain gets 5 emails/day regardless of ambition. Targets include daily email count, weekly meetings, and reply rate.
    73→
    74→11. **Generate Skills** — All 16 project-local skills are generated from the collected data. Skills at this level acknowledge they're conversation-derived and will improve with data.
    75→
    76→12. **Live Demo** — Without announcing it as a demo, Claude researches a real company, finds a matching persona, drafts a cold email (50-90 words), and offers to send it. Setup is marked complete.
    77→
    78→### Experimenting (Some signal, growing)
    79→
    80→**Who gets here:** Users with some outbound emails plus tools, or 200-499 outbound emails regardless of tools, or 500+ emails but no CRM.
    81→
    82→**Philosophy:** Learn from what exists, fill gaps. They have signal — use it, but don't assume it's clean or intentional.
    83→
    84→**15 steps (E1-E15):**
    85→
    86→Steps are similar to Zero but enhanced with email data analysis:
    87→
    88→- **E1** presents classified email results from Phase 1
    89→- **E2-E3** check sending infrastructure and deliverability, cross-referencing against actual sending volume
    90→- **E4** connects CRM if available (currently HubSpot only)
    91→- **E5** builds ICP from a hybrid of email patterns and user input ("You've been emailing Series B SaaS companies — intentional or casting wide?")
    92→- **E6** extracts buyer personas from email patterns (which titles they email most)
    93→- **E7** compares product positioning between website and actual email language
    94→- **E8** sources social proof from website, email history, and user input
    95→- **E9** qualification framework informed by any CRM deal data
    96→- **E10** prospect geography inferred from email recipients
    97→- **E11** light pattern analysis (reply patterns, subject line patterns, send time patterns)
    98→- **E12** KPIs based on current volume plus infrastructure constraints
    99→- **E13** gap analysis showing where Nurge fills vs. enhances existing tools
   100→- **E14** generates all 16 skills as hybrid (data + conversation)
   101→- **E15** live demo using patterns from their actual data
   102→
   103→### Operating (Real data, real tools, solo)
   104→
   105→**Who gets here:** Users with 500+ outbound emails, a CRM, working solo or small team (size = 1).
   106→
   107→**Philosophy:** Data-first. Show them things they don't know about their own outbound. The value is the intelligence layer.
   108→
   109→**13 steps (O1-O13):**
   110→
   111→This flow is heavily data-driven. Key differences:
   112→
   113→- **O2** presents full email history analysis as the "goldmine" — total outbound, unique companies, top personas, volume trend
   114→- **O4** is the "aha moment" — ICP inferred from real patterns, comparing who they actually email vs. who responds vs. who converts. Often reveals misalignment ("You think Series B is your sweet spot, but your best replies come from Series C")
   115→- **O5** shows persona performance with real metrics ("You email CTOs most, but VP Engineering has 2x higher reply rate")
   116→- **O6** compares positioning in emails vs. website — suggests alignment
   117→- **O7** identifies proof points used in actual emails ("You drop Acme Corp in 40% of your emails — is that your strongest proof point?")
   118→- **O8** runs full performance analysis: reply rates by persona, subject line patterns, optimal send times, sequence analysis, segment insights
   119→- **O10** sets data-informed KPI targets with benchmarks
   120→- **O12** generates data-derived skills that are "immediately strong"
   121→- **O13** leads with a data insight then drafts from winning patterns
   122→
   123→### Scaling (Team with mature process)
   124→
   125→**Who gets here:** Teams (size > 1) with 500+ outbound emails and CRM.
   126→
   127→**Philosophy:** Team-wide intelligence. Cross-rep analysis, consistency, best-practice sharing.
   128→
   129→**14 steps (S1-S14):**
   130→
   131→Adds team-specific dimensions:
   132→
   133→- **S2** maps team structure: rep count, territory splits, shared vs. individual mailboxes, top performer
   134→- **S4** audits deliverability across all team sending domains
   135→- **S5-S6** segment ICP and persona performance by rep, surfacing cross-rep insights
   136→- **S7** checks positioning consistency across the team
   137→- **S8** builds a shared proof point library
   138→- **S9** includes per-rep performance breakdowns and best-practice extraction from top performers
   139→- **S11** sets both team-level and per-rep KPI targets
   140→- **S13** generates team-aware skills that reference team structure and cross-rep patterns
   141→- **S14** demos with a team-level insight ("Your team's CTO conversion is X% — 2x industry average. But VP Eng lags...")
   142→
   143→**Current limitation:** Email sync works for one Gmail account. Multi-mailbox sync is not yet built. Team insights come from CRM pipeline data and the connected mailbox, supplemented by conversation.
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## The Skill Generation Step
   148→
   149→Regardless of which Phase 2 flow runs, they all reach a "Generate Skills" step that follows the same process:
   150→
   151→1. Claude reads the skill generation guide (a master instruction file embedded in the setup skill)
   152→2. Claude reads the current project config via `nurge config show --format json`
   153→3. For each of the 16 skills, Claude generates a SKILL.md file containing:
   154→   - YAML frontmatter (name, description, version)
   155→   - Complete instructions for that skill's task
   156→   - References to relevant config keys
   157→   - CLI commands the skill uses
   158→   - Maturity-appropriate language and confidence level
   159→   - Cross-references to related skills
   160→4. Each file is written to `.claude/skills/nurge-<name>/SKILL.md`
   161→5. The version is recorded in the database with metadata (email count, learning count)
   162→
   163→The generation guide specifies exact requirements per skill — for example, the outbound skill must enforce 50-90 word limits with word count validation, the research skill must dispatch 5 parallel agents, and the qualify skill must classify leads as prospect/competitor/partnership before scoring.
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→## Resume and Idempotency
   168→
   169→Setup can be interrupted and resumed at any point. Every step saves its output to config, and the router checks which config keys exist before deciding where to continue. If a user disconnects during persona definition and comes back later, setup picks up from that exact step.
   170→
   171→The setup skill itself (the router) checks three things on every invocation:
   172→1. Is `setup_completed` true? If yes, don't re-run.
   173→2. Is `maturity.level` set? If yes, Phase 1 is done — jump to the right Phase 2 file.
   174→3. Otherwise, check individual Phase 1 config keys and skip completed steps.
   175→
   176→---
   177→
   178→## What Triggers Skill Regeneration
   179→
   180→Skills can be regenerated after setup in several scenarios:
   181→- More email data syncs (more patterns to learn from)
   182→- User provides feedback that accumulates as learnings
   183→- User explicitly asks for skills to be refreshed
   184→- A periodic review suggests skills are stale (many new learnings not yet applied)
   185→
   186→Regeneration follows the same generation guide but with updated config and learnings, producing a new version while preserving the ability to roll back.
   187→

1→# Maturity Levels
     2→
     3→Nurge detects each user's outbound maturity during setup and tailors every skill to match. The same skill (say, nurge-icp) contains different language, confidence levels, and data sources depending on whether the user is starting from scratch or running a mature team operation.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## The Four Levels
     8→
     9→### Zero — Starting from Scratch
    10→
    11→**Profile:** Fewer than 200 outbound emails and no CRM. Building from nothing.
    12→
    13→**How they get here:** The detection algorithm checks outbound email count (under 200) and CRM status (none). If someone claims a CRM but hasn't connected it, they're treated as Zero.
    14→
    15→**Skill characteristics:**
    16→- Skills are conversation-derived — built from setup discussion and website scrape, not from email data
    17→- Language acknowledges uncertainty: "based on what we discussed", "this will sharpen as you send more emails"
    18→- ICP, personas, and positioning are assumption-based, confirmed by the user
    19→- Infrastructure is prioritized over messaging (deliverability must be validated before sending)
    20→- Conservative KPIs: 5 emails/day for fresh domains, 1-2 replies/week, 1 meeting/week
    21→- nurge-prospects is critical because it's their only prospect management system
    22→
    23→**Setup flow (12 steps):** Focuses on building infrastructure (domain, DNS, warmup) before moving to targeting (ICP, personas, positioning). Qualification uses a default BANT framework. Goals are modest.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→### Experimenting — Growing with Some Signal
    28→
    29→**Profile:** One of these situations:
    30→- Some outbound emails plus existing tools (any count, but has CRM)
    31→- 200-499 outbound emails regardless of tools
    32→- 500+ emails but no CRM (lots of activity but no system)
    33→
    34→**How they get here:** The "growing zone" — enough data to learn from but not enough for deep pattern analysis. Users with tools but little data land here too, since having a CRM signals process awareness.
    35→
    36→**Skill characteristics:**
    37→- Skills use a hybrid approach — real data where it exists, assumptions where it doesn't
    38→- Language references specific patterns from data alongside gaps: "I can see you email CTOs most, but I'm guessing at their pain points"
    39→- ICP combines email pattern analysis with user input
    40→- Personas extracted from email recipients but supplemented with AI suggestions
    41→- Product positioning compared between website and actual email language
    42→- Social proof sourced from website, email content, and user input
    43→- Light pattern analysis (subject lines, send times, reply rates) with honest caveats about limited data
    44→
    45→**Setup flow (15 steps):** Starts by presenting what exists (classified email results), then builds on it. Includes CRM connection if applicable, gap analysis showing where Nurge fills in for missing tools.
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→### Operating — Data-Rich Solo Operator
    50→
    51→**Profile:** 500+ outbound emails, CRM in place, working solo or very small team (team size = 1).
    52→
    53→**How they get here:** Rich email data plus tools. The threshold of 500 outbound emails means there's enough signal for meaningful pattern analysis.
    54→
    55→**Skill characteristics:**
    56→- Skills are data-derived — reference specific patterns from real email analysis
    57→- Language is confident and insight-driven: "Your CTO emails convert at 18% — 2x your VP Eng rate"
    58→- ICP inferred from actual patterns, often revealing misalignment between stated targets and real performance
    59→- Personas include real reply rate data and per-persona messaging analysis
    60→- Product positioning compared across channels (email vs. website), with recommendations for alignment
    61→- Social proof identified from actual usage patterns in emails ("You mention Acme Corp in 40% of your outbound")
    62→- Full performance analysis: reply rates by persona, subject line patterns, optimal send times, sequence analysis
    63→- KPIs set against real benchmarks with current vs. target vs. industry comparisons
    64→
    65→**Setup flow (13 steps):** Heavily data-driven. The ICP step is the "aha moment" — showing users things they didn't know about their own outbound. Full performance analysis runs before KPI setting so targets are informed by reality.
    66→
    67→---
    68→
    69→### Scaling — Team with Mature Process
    70→
    71→**Profile:** Team of 2+ people, 500+ outbound emails, CRM in place.
    72→
    73→**How they get here:** Everything Operating has, plus a team. The additional signal is team structure and cross-rep analysis.
    74→
    75→**Skill characteristics:**
    76→- Skills are data-derived plus team-aware — reference team structure, territories, and per-rep patterns
    77→- Language surfaces cross-team insights: "Your team's CTO conversion is 15% — 2x average. But VP Eng lags at 3%."
    78→- ICP segmented by rep to surface targeting inconsistencies
    79→- Personas include per-rep performance data and best-practice extraction from top performers
    80→- Positioning checked for consistency across the team — flags drift if reps describe the product differently
    81→- Social proof curated as a shared library the whole team draws from
    82→- Analytics include team-level and per-rep breakdowns
    83→- KPIs set at both team and individual levels
    84→- Review skill includes per-rep coaching recommendations
    85→
    86→**Current limitation:** Email sync works for one Gmail account. Multi-mailbox sync is not yet built. Team insights come from CRM pipeline data and the connected mailbox, supplemented by conversation about team structure. Each rep can connect their own account separately (future feature).
    87→
    88→**Setup flow (14 steps):** Includes team structure mapping, multi-domain deliverability audit, cross-rep analysis from CRM data, shared playbook generation.
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## Detection Algorithm
    93→
    94→Maturity is detected automatically during Phase 1, step F7, using three signals:
    95→
    96→| Outbound Count | Has CRM | Team Size | Result |
    97→|---|---|---|---|
    98→| Under 200 | No | Any | Zero |
    99→| Under 200 | Yes (not connected) | Any | Zero |
   100→| Under 200 | Yes (connected) | Any | Experimenting |
   101→| 200-499 | Any | Any | Experimenting |
   102→| 500+ | No | Any | Experimenting |
   103→| 500+ | Yes | 1 | Operating |
   104→| 500+ | Yes | 2+ | Scaling |
   105→
   106→**Key thresholds:**
   107→- Under 200 outbound emails: not enough data for pattern analysis
   108→- 200-499: growing zone, data exists but isn't rich enough for Operating-level insights
   109→- 500+: rich data, level depends on tools and team
   110→
   111→The detected level is presented to the user in plain language for confirmation. Users can override the detection — their choice is respected and saved.
   112→
   113→**Override examples:**
   114→- User says "starting fresh" but has 400+ outbound → bumped to Experimenting: "Looks like you've sent some outbound already. Let me learn from that."
   115→- User feels they're more advanced than detected → their override is saved
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## How Maturity Affects Each Skill
   120→
   121→| Skill | Zero | Experimenting | Operating | Scaling |
   122→|---|---|---|---|---|
   123→| nurge-product | Website + conversation | Website + email language | Email language analysis | Team-wide messaging consistency |
   124→| nurge-icp | User assumptions | Email patterns + assumptions | Data-derived, reveals misalignment | Per-rep segmentation |
   125→| nurge-personas | AI suggestions | Email recipient analysis + AI | Real reply rate data per persona | Cross-rep persona performance |
   126→| nurge-research | Full research (no prior history) | Checks prior interactions | Rich prior interaction data | Team-wide interaction check |
   127→| nurge-qualify | Default BANT | BANT + conservative defaults | Pipeline-informed framework | Deal velocity analysis |
   128→| nurge-outbound | Assumption-based targeting | Hybrid targeting | Data-driven patterns | Top performer patterns |
   129→| nurge-sequence | Best-practice 3-touch | Best-practice + light data | Performance-informed cadences | Cross-rep cadence optimization |
   130→| nurge-prospects | Critical (only CRM) | Important (partial CRM) | Supplementary to CRM | Team prospect coordination |
   131→| nurge-analytics | Basic volume metrics | Light pattern analysis | Full breakdown by dimension | Per-rep breakdowns |
   132→| nurge-review | Getting-started coaching | Habit-building focus | Data-driven recommendations | Per-rep coaching |
   133→| nurge-sending-health | Infrastructure setup focus | Volume vs. capacity checks | Health monitoring | Multi-domain audit |
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## Maturity Progression
   138→
   139→Users don't stay at one level forever. As they send more emails, connect more tools, and grow their team, their skills can be regenerated at a higher maturity level. The system tracks how much data existed when each skill was generated (email count, learning count), making it possible to know when skills are stale relative to available data.
   140→
   141→Typical progression:
   142→1. Zero user starts sending, accumulates 200+ emails → skills regenerated at Experimenting level
   143→2. Experimenting user hits 500+ emails, connects CRM → skills regenerated at Operating level
   144→3. Operating user grows team → skills regenerated at Scaling level
   145→
   146→Each regeneration preserves all previous versions in the database with rollback capability.
   147→

1→# Config Reference
     2→
     3→Every skill reads its data from the project config via `nurge config show --format json`. This document lists every config key, what it stores, when it's set, and which skills depend on it.
     4→
     5→Config is stored in `.nurge/config.json` as a flat key-value map under the `settings` field.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## User Identity
    10→
    11→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    12→|---|---|---|---|
    13→| `user.name` | The user's preferred name | Phase 1, step F1 | nurge-review, all skills (personalization) |
    14→| `user.role` | Job title/role (founder, head of sales, SDR, AE) | Phase 1, step F3 | Maturity detection, skill tone |
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Company Information
    19→
    20→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    21→|---|---|---|---|
    22→| `company.website` | Company website URL | Phase 1, step F2 | nurge-product |
    23→| `company.description` | One-line company description | Phase 1, step F2 | nurge-product, nurge-qualify |
    24→| `company.industry` | Industry/vertical | Phase 1, step F2 | nurge-qualify |
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Product
    29→
    30→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    31→|---|---|---|---|
    32→| `product.positioning` | Punchy one-liner for cold outreach (under 15 words) | Phase 2, Z7/E7/O6/S7 | nurge-outbound, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-book, nurge-research |
    33→| `product.social_proof` | JSON array of proof points (logos, testimonials, metrics) or "none" | Phase 2, Z8/E8/O7/S8 | nurge-outbound |
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## Ideal Customer Profile
    38→
    39→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    40→|---|---|---|---|
    41→| `icp.company_size` | Target company size range (employees or revenue) | Phase 2, Z5/E5/O4/S5 | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
    42→| `icp.industry` | Target industries/verticals | Phase 2, Z5/E5/O4/S5 | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
    43→| `icp.stage` | Target company stage (startup, growth, enterprise) | Phase 2, Z5/E5/O4/S5 | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
    44→| `icp.geography` | Target geography | Phase 2, Z5/E5/O4/S5 | nurge-icp, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects |
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→## Personas
    49→
    50→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    51→|---|---|---|---|
    52→| `personas` | JSON array of buyer personas (title, pain points, messaging angle, tone) | Phase 2, Z6/E6/O5/S6 | nurge-personas, nurge-outbound, nurge-sequence, nurge-followup, nurge-replies, nurge-book, nurge-research, nurge-qualify, nurge-prospects, nurge-analytics, nurge-review |
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## Prospects
    57→
    58→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    59→|---|---|---|---|
    60→| `prospects.geography` | Where prospects are located (US, EU, Global, specific regions) | Phase 2, Z4/E10/inferred | nurge-icp, nurge-outbound, nurge-research, nurge-prospects, nurge-hygiene |
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→## Qualification
    65→
    66→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    67→|---|---|---|---|
    68→| `qualification.framework` | Qualification method (default: BANT) with criteria | Phase 2, Z9/E9/O9/S10 | nurge-qualify, nurge-replies |
    69→
    70→---
    71→
    72→## Sending Infrastructure
    73→
    74→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    75→|---|---|---|---|
    76→| `sending.domain_type` | "dedicated" or "primary" | Phase 2, Z1/E2/O3 | nurge-sending-health |
    77→| `sending.mailbox_count` | Number of email accounts used for sending | Phase 2, Z1/E2 | nurge-sending-health |
    78→| `sending.warmup_status` | "warmed" or "fresh" | Phase 2, Z1/E2/O3 | nurge-sending-health, nurge-pipeline |
    79→| `sending.deliverability_checked` | Boolean — DNS checks completed | Phase 2, Z2/E3/O3/S4 | Resume logic |
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## KPIs and Goals
    84→
    85→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    86→|---|---|---|---|
    87→| `kpi.goal` | Primary outbound goal (book meetings, build pipeline, generate revenue, validate PMF) | Phase 2, Z10/E12/O10/S11 | nurge-pipeline, nurge-analytics, nurge-review |
    88→| `kpi.daily_emails` | Daily email target | Phase 2, Z10/E12/O10/S11 | nurge-pipeline, nurge-sequence, nurge-sending-health, nurge-analytics, nurge-review |
    89→| `kpi.weekly_meetings` | Weekly meeting target | Phase 2, Z10/E12/O10/S11 | nurge-analytics, nurge-review |
    90→| `kpi.reply_rate_target` | Target reply rate (percentage) | Phase 2, Z10/E12/O10/S11 | nurge-analytics, nurge-review |
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Team
    95→
    96→| Key | What It Stores | Set During | Used By |
    97→|---|---|---|---|

1→# Versioning and Learning
     2→
     3→Skills are not static. They improve over time through two mechanisms: accumulated learnings from user conversations, and regeneration from growing email data. Every version is tracked in the database with full rollback capability.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## The Learning System
     8→
     9→### What Gets Captured
    10→
    11→Every Claude Code conversation is a learning opportunity. The system captures six types of insights:
    12→
    13→| Type | What It Captures | Example |
    14→|---|---|---|
    15→| tone | Email tone preferences | "Too salesy — make it more casual" |
    16→| icp | ICP refinements | "We don't sell to startups under 50 people" |
    17→| product | Product positioning tweaks | "Don't mention pricing in cold emails" |
    18→| pattern | Email patterns discovered | "CTOs respond better to technical angles" |
    19→| competitor | Competitor intel mentioned | "Acme just raised Series C, they're expanding into our space" |
    20→| preference | Any explicit user correction | "Always use their first name, never Mr./Ms." |
    21→
    22→### How Capture Works
    23→
    24→Nurge installs hooks into Claude Code that fire during every conversation:
    25→
    26→1. **Session Start** — Creates a session record in the database. Injects context (current KPIs, available data, sending constraints) so Claude has fresh state.
    27→
    28→2. **Prompt Submission** — Logs each user prompt as a session event.
    29→
    30→3. **Stop** — Records which files were modified during the conversation as a checkpoint.
    31→
    32→4. **Session End** — Marks the session as complete.
    33→
    34→Skills can save learnings explicitly during a conversation by calling:
    35→```
    36→nurge learn save --type tone --stdin
    37→```
    38→
    39→The learning text is read from standard input, tagged with its type and source session, and stored in the database.
    40→
    41→### How Learnings Feed Back Into Skills
    42→
    43→When skills are regenerated, Claude reads all accumulated learnings via `nurge learn list --format json` and incorporates them into the new skill content. For example:
    44→
    45→- 5 tone learnings saying "more casual" → the outbound skill's tone guidance shifts toward casual
    46→- An ICP learning saying "no startups under 50" → the ICP skill adds this as a negative filter
    47→- A product learning saying "don't mention pricing" → the outbound skill excludes pricing from email templates
    48→
    49→The learning count is recorded with each skill version, so it's always clear how many learnings were applied.
    50→
    51→### Content Draft Tracking
    52→
    53→The system also tracks how users edit AI-generated content:
    54→
    55→- **AI draft** — What Claude originally wrote
    56→- **User final** — What the user actually sent (after edits)
    57→- **Diff** — A computed summary of changes (lines added, removed, changed)
    58→- **Content type** — Whether it was an email, skill, or other content
    59→
    60→This data reveals patterns in how users modify Claude's output, which informs future skill improvements.
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→## Skill Versioning
    65→
    66→### How Versions Work
    67→
    68→Every skill can have multiple versions stored in the database. Only one version is "active" at any time — that's the one written to disk as SKILL.md for Claude to read.
    69→
    70→Each version records:
    71→- **Version number** — Auto-increments per skill (v1, v2, v3...)
    72→- **Content** — The full SKILL.md text
    73→- **Email count** — How many emails were synced when this version was generated
    74→- **Learning count** — How many learnings were applied to this version
    75→- **Active flag** — Whether this is the version currently on disk
    76→- **Timestamp** — When this version was created
    77→
    78→### When New Versions Are Created
    79→
    80→A new version is created whenever a skill is regenerated. This happens:
    81→- During initial setup (version 1 for all skills)
    82→- When the user explicitly asks for skills to be refreshed
    83→- When a periodic review suggests skills are stale
    84→- When maturity level changes (e.g., user graduates from Experimenting to Operating)
    85→
    86→### The Version Record
    87→
    88→When a new version is saved:
    89→1. The system finds the next version number (max existing + 1)
    90→2. All previous versions for that skill are deactivated
    91→3. The new version is inserted with active = 1
    92→4. The content is written to disk at `.claude/skills/nurge-<name>/SKILL.md`
    93→
    94→Previous versions remain in the database — they're deactivated, not deleted.
    95→
    96→---
    97→
    98→## Rollback
    99→
   100→If a regeneration makes a skill worse (e.g., the new outbound skill produces emails the user doesn't like), any previous version can be restored.
   101→
   102→**How rollback works:**
   103→1. The system verifies the target version exists in the database
   104→2. All versions for that skill are deactivated
   105→3. The target version is marked as active
   106→4. The target version's content is written back to disk, replacing the current SKILL.md
   107→
   108→**User commands:**
   109→
   110→View version history:
   111→```
   112→nurge skills history nurge-outbound
   113→```
   114→Shows all versions with timestamp, email count, and learning count. The active version is marked.
   115→
   116→Restore a previous version:
   117→```
   118→nurge skills rollback nurge-outbound 2
   119→```
   120→Restores version 2 to disk and marks it as active.
   121→
   122→List all skills with version info:
   123→```
   124→nurge skills list
   125→```
   126→Shows each skill's active version number, total version count, and last update time.
   127→
   128→---
   129→
   130→## Why the Database Owns Versioning (Not Git)
   131→
   132→Users of Nurge are salespeople and founders — they don't use git, don't commit files, and don't manage branches. Git is used internally for the Nurge codebase, but skill versioning is handled entirely by the database because:
   133→
   134→1. **Users never interact with git** — Rollback is a single CLI command, not a git operation
   135→2. **Metadata matters** — Each version carries email count and learning count, showing what data informed it
   136→3. **Disk is ephemeral** — The SKILL.md on disk is just the active version. The database holds the full history.
   137→4. **Comparison is easy** — Version metadata shows what changed between regenerations (more emails? more learnings? higher maturity?)
   138→
   139→The pattern is: database owns history, disk is a projection of the active version.
   140→
   141→---
   142→
   143→## Staleness Detection
   144→
   145→Skills can become stale when:
   146→- Many new emails have been synced since the skill was generated (email count has grown significantly)
   147→- Many new learnings have accumulated since the skill was generated (learning count has grown)
   148→- The user's maturity level has changed (e.g., crossed the 500-email threshold)
   149→- Config values have changed (new ICP, updated personas, revised KPIs)
   150→
   151→The system can detect staleness by comparing:
   152→- Current email count vs. the email count recorded in the active skill version
   153→- Current learning count vs. the learning count recorded in the active skill version
   154→- Current maturity level vs. the level the skill was generated for
   155→
   156→This information is surfaced during session start (via the rotating dashboard banner) and during periodic reviews.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## Session Tracking
   161→
   162→Beyond learnings and skill versions, the system tracks full session metadata:
   163→
   164→**Sessions table** — Every Claude Code conversation gets a record with: session ID, model used, transcript path, start time, end time, and status.
   165→
   166→**Session events** — Each significant action within a session (tool use, prompt, stop) is logged with type and payload.
   167→
   168→**Session checkpoints** — When the user stops Claude, any modified files are recorded as a checkpoint.
   169→
   170→This data supports:
   171→- Understanding how the user interacts with Nurge over time
   172→- Tracing which session generated which learning
   173→- Identifying patterns in user behavior that could improve the product
   174→

1→package cmd
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"encoding/json"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"os"
     7→
     8→	"github.com/spf13/cobra"
     9→	"github.com/usenurge/nurgesh/internal/account"
    10→)
    11→
    12→var accountFormat string
    13→
    14→var accountCmd = &cobra.Command{
    15→	Use:         "account",
    16→	Short:       "Show account status",
    17→	Annotations: map[string]string{"skipAuth": "true"},
    18→	RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    19→		af, err := account.LoadAuth()
    20→		if err != nil {
    21→			if accountFormat == "json" {
    22→				data, _ := json.Marshal(map[string]any{"logged_in": false})
    23→				fmt.Println(string(data))
    24→				return nil
    25→			}
    26→			fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "  Not logged in. Run `nurge account login` to authenticate.")
    27→			return nil
    28→		}
    29→
    30→		if accountFormat == "json" {
    31→			data, _ := json.Marshal(map[string]any{
    32→				"logged_in": true,
    33→				"email":     af.Email,
    34→				"name":      af.Name,
    35→			})
    36→			fmt.Println(string(data))
    37→			return nil
    38→		}
    39→
    40→		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "  Logged in as %s (%s)\n", af.Name, af.Email)
    41→		return nil
    42→	},
    43→}
    44→
    45→var accountLoginCmd = &cobra.Command{
    46→	Use:         "login",
    47→	Short:       "Log in to your nurge account",
    48→	Annotations: map[string]string{"skipAuth": "true"},
    49→	RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    50→		_, err := account.Login(GetConvexURL(), GetDashboardURL())
    51→		return err
    52→	},
    53→}
    54→
    55→var accountLogoutCmd = &cobra.Command{
    56→	Use:         "logout",
    57→	Short:       "Log out of your nurge account",
    58→	Annotations: map[string]string{"skipAuth": "true"},
    59→	RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    60→		return account.Logout(GetConvexURL())
    61→	},
    62→}
    63→
    64→func init() {
    65→	accountCmd.PersistentFlags().StringVar(&accountFormat, "format", "", "Output format (json)")
    66→	accountCmd.AddCommand(accountLoginCmd)
    67→	accountCmd.AddCommand(accountLogoutCmd)
    68→	rootCmd.AddCommand(accountCmd)
    69→}

     3→In version 1, we want users to add their own GCP GMAIL creds to facilitate send, I dont have it. Can this be automated?
     4→Test the flow from creating context, ICP, enrichment and send emails.
     5→Skills for editting nurge skills using claude write.

    10→Sequencer status: Nurge can already send real emails through the Gmail API.
    11→Sequences work locally today with enrollments, tasks, reply sync, suppressions, and mailbox caps.
    12→`nurge send --sequence ...` creates the first touch and schedules the next follow-up.
    13→`nurge dispatcher run --execute` can send ready follow-ups automatically from the local queue.
    14→`nurge dispatcher install` now generates the local cron/launchd wrapper for unattended execution.
    15→The built-in `test-3-touch-1m` template is useful for fast end-to-end testing.
    16→The remaining gap is true remote execution when the user's machine is off.
    17→We also need a stronger reply-needed inbox flow, not just reply stop/pause logic.
    18→Real multi-mailbox auth and transport selection are still needed for true rotation.
    19→Sequence templates, campaigns, and lead import/dedupe should become first-class CLI objects.
    20→Longer term, Convex + Unipile can make it feel closer to Smartlead with remote execution.
